7 min read

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Deploying a modern web application into production can feel like a daunting puzzle. There are server configurations to manage, security holes to patch, databases to secure, and once all of that is done, automated pipelines to build.
Recently, I built a custom Marine Portal Application using PHP 8.1 and MySQL. Instead of relying on complex, over-engineered cloud setups, I chose AWS Lightsail for its simplicity, predictable pricing, and high performance.
Here is the step-by-step journal of how I took my application from a local GitHub repository to a hardened, fully automated production environment using Nginx, Let’s Encrypt SSL, and GitHub Actions CI/CD.
The Architecture Overview
Before diving into the code, here is what the target architecture looks like:

• Hosting: AWS Lightsail (Ubuntu 22.04 LTS) with a Static IP.

• Web Server: Nginx acting as a reverse proxy, serving PHP-FPM 8.1.

• Database: MySQL Server locked down to local connections only.

• Security: Let’s Encrypt SSL (HTTPS), strict ufw firewall, and hardened SSH keys.

• CI/CD: GitHub Actions executing secure SSH deployments only after passing automated security scans.
Phase 1: Provisioning the AWS Lightsail Instance
I started by spinning up an Ubuntu 22.04 LTS instance on AWS Lightsail. Ubuntu 22.04 is a fantastic choice for PHP 8.1 because it offers excellent stability and package support.
To prevent my server’s IP address from changing every time the system reboots, I instantly provisioned and attached a Static IP.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Next, I opened the Lightsail firewall settings and restricted incoming traffic to only three vital ports:

• SSH (Port 22)

• HTTP (Port 80)

• HTTPS (Port 443)
Phase 2: Installing the LNMP Stack & PHP 8.1
With SSH access ready, I updated the package manager and installed Nginx, Git, and Unzip:
sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade -y
sudo apt install nginx git unzip curl -yOvercoming the PHP 8.1 Package Hurdle
Depending on your exact OS minor version, sometimes running sudo apt install php8.1 throws a frustrating Unable to locate package error. To solve this, I imported the industry-standard Ondřej Surý PHP PPA, which hosts optimized, co-installable PHP builds for Debian/Ubuntu:
# Install prerequisites for repository management
sudo apt install -y software-properties-common ca-certificates lsb-release apt-transport-https# Add the trusted PHP PPA
sudo add-apt-repository ppa:ondrej/php -y
sudo apt update
Once the catalog was updated, installing PHP 8.1 and its primary extensions was a breeze:
sudo apt install php8.1-fpm php8.1-cli php8.1-mysql php8.1-xml php8.1-curl php8.1-mbstring php8.1-zip -yPhase 3: Database & Codebase Setup
Setting Up and Securing MySQL
Next, I installed MySQL Server and secured it right out of the box using the mysql_secure_installation Utility to remove default accounts and enforce strong password policies.
sudo apt install mysql-server -y
sudo mysql_secure_installationInside MySQL, I created an isolated database and a unique user with restricted privileges, ensuring the application didn’t run using the root account:
CREATE DATABASE marine_db CHARACTER SET utf8mb4 COLLATE utf8mb4_unicode_ci;
CREATE USER 'marine_user'@'localhost' IDENTIFIED BY 'your_strong_password';
GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON marine_db.* TO 'marine_user'@'localhost';
FLUSH PRIVILEGES;Cloning the Codebase via SSH Deploy Keys
Rather than exposing my personal GitHub password, I generated a dedicated SSH key pair on the server and added the public key as a Deploy Key on GitHub.
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "your-email@example.com"
cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub # Added this to GitHub Repo SettingsLog in to your GitHub account, click on the repository name, e.g marine_rep, click on setting you will deploy the key on GitHub
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Configure directory permissions so Nginx can read and execute files:
sudo chown -r www-data:www-data /var/www/marine-app
sudo find /var/www/marine-app -type f -exec chmod 644 {} \;
sudo find /var/www/marine-app -type d -exec chmod 755 {} \;find .: Tells the system to search through the current directory
and all of its subfolders.
-type f: Filters the search to target only files (ignoring folders).
-exec chmod 644 {} \;: Changes the permissions of every file found to 644.
Why? 644 means the owner (ubuntu) can read and write to the files,
while the web server (www-data) and external visitors can only read them.
This prevents anyone from executing malicious scripts on your raw files.I then cloned the repository into the /var/www/ directory:
sudo git clone git@github.com:your-username/marine-app.git /var/www/marine-appPhase 4: Nginx Configuration & Let’s Encrypt SSL
To point incoming domain requests to our PHP application, I created a custom server block configuration:
$ cd /etc/nginx/sites-available/
$ sudo vim marine-app #you can use nano much simplierserver {
listen 80;
server_name yourdomain.com www.yourdomain.com; //replace with address only if you dont have a domain name
root /var/www/marine-app; index index.php index.html index.htm;
location / {
try_files $uri $uri/ /index.php?$query_string;
}
location ~ \.php$ {
include snippets/fastcgi-php.conf;
fastcgi_pass unix:/var/run/php/php8.1-fpm.sock;
}
location ~ /\.ht {
deny all;
}
}
After pasting this configuration above, you need to enable the site configuration and test it for syntax errors
sudo ln -s /etc/nginx/sites-available/marine-app /etc/nginx/sites-enabled/
sudo rm /etc/nginx/sites-enabled/default #this is optional
sudo nginx -t #to test the configuration
sudo systemctl reload nginx #to reload nginx serverAt this point, if you run your application on the browser using the IP address, you have a working app on your browser.
I installed Certbot to automatically issue and configure a free Let’s Encrypt SSL certificate:
sudo apt install certbot python3-certbot-nginx -y
sudo certbot --nginx -d yourdomain.com -d www.yourdomain.comPhase 5: Hardening Security
In production, security is a continuous job, not a checkbox. I implemented three vital steps to protect my application:

• Hardened php.ini: I turned off expose_php so attackers can't see what PHP version I am running, restricted dangerous system execution functions (like exec and shell_exec), and forced cookies to only transmit over HTTPS (session.cookie_secure = On).

• Environment Variable Isolation: The production .env containing my MySQL credentials was kept strictly on the server and added to .gitignore So it could never leak on GitHub.

• Strict File Permissions: I structured directory permissions so that my deployer user (ubuntu) can write code, while the web server user (www-data) has read-only access to run the app.
Phase 6: Automated CI/CD with GitHub Actions
With the app running, the manual git pull workflow became tedious. I built an automated CI/CD pipeline using GitHub Actions.
Setting Up Secret Keys
On GitHub, I configured three repository secrets to keep my server credentials safe:
On that same github repository click on the secret and variable dropdown and select action and add the key generated on your server
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Press enter or click to view image in full size

• SSH_HOST (My Lightsail Static IP)

• SSH_USERNAME (ubuntu)

• SSH_KEY (A dedicated private SSH key generated specifically for GitHub Actions)
The Hardened CI/CD Workflow
I wanted my pipeline to be secure. If a developer accidentally pushes an API key, or if Composer pulls a dependency with a known security vulnerability, the pipeline must block the deployment.
I created .github/workflows/deploy.yml with three logical stages:

• Security Scans: Checks PHP dependencies for vulnerabilities using Symfony’s Security Checker and runs gitleaks to check for hardcoded secrets.

• Static Code Analysis: Analyzes the quality of the PHP code.

• Secure Deployment: Connects over SSH, pulls the code, and re-applies safe permissions.
Here is the exact YAML configuration:
name: Secure Build & Deployon:
push:
branches:
- main
jobs:
security-scans:
name: Run Security Audits
runs-on: ubuntu-latest
steps:
- name: Checkout Code
uses: actions/checkout@v4
with:
fetch-depth: 0
# 1. Check for vulnerable dependencies
- name: PHP Security Checker
uses: symfonycorp/security-checker-action@v5
# 2. Check for hardcoded secrets or API keys
- name: Secret Scanning (Gitleaks)
uses: gitleaks/gitleaks-action@v2
env:
GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }} # Generated automatically by GitHub!
code-quality:
name: Code Static Analysis
runs-on: ubuntu-latest
steps:
- name: Checkout Code
uses: actions/checkout@v4
- name: Setup PHP
uses: shivammathur/setup-php@v2
with:
php-version: '8.1'
- name: Install Dependencies
run: composer install --no-progress --no-suggest --prefer-dist
deploy:
name: Deploy to Lightsail
needs: [security-scans, code-quality] # Deploy ONLY if audits pass!
runs-on: ubuntu-latest
steps:
- name: Checkout Code
uses: actions/checkout@v4
- name: Execute Remote SSH Commands
uses: appleboy/ssh-action@v1.0.3
with:
host: ${{ secrets.SSH_HOST }}
username: ${{ secrets.SSH_USERNAME }}
key: ${{ secrets.SSH_KEY }}
port: 22
script: |
cd /var/www/marine-app
git pull origin main
# Secure file permissions
find . -type f -exec chmod 644 {} \;
find . -type d -exec chmod 755 {} \;
Conclusion
Deploying an application using a custom-configured stack is one of the best learning experiences a developer can have. By bypassing bloated hosting panels and configuring Nginx, PHP 8.1, and MySQL by hand on AWS Lightsail, I gained absolute control over my application’s environment.
With a hardened server configuration and a secure CI/CD pipeline running checks on every push, I can now focus on what matters most: building robust features for my marine app.
Up next, I will be explaining how to deploy and run CI/CD for a React/Node.js TypeScript app using Nginx and proxy pass.
